title: Bottleneck Analyzer Guide
summary: Understand which system resource is most likely limiting performance and where to focus design review.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool helps identify the dominant performance constraint across CPU, memory, disk, network, queueing, or utilization pressure.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch performance constraints before they become user-facing problems.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when a system feels slow, when comparing upgrade options, or when deciding whether performance problems are caused by compute, storage, network, or load behavior.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system troubleshooting.
Explaining performance tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical performance planning terms.

items.label: CPU Pressure
items.description: How heavily the processor is being used under the workload.
items.whyItMatters: CPU pressure can limit throughput even when other resources look fine.
items.commonMistake: Upgrading storage or network while CPU remains the real bottleneck.

items.label: Memory Pressure
items.description: How close the system is to exhausting available RAM.
items.whyItMatters: Memory pressure can cause slowdowns that look like disk or application problems.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring memory because the system has not fully crashed.

items.label: Disk Pressure
items.description: How heavily the storage layer is being used.
items.whyItMatters: Storage pressure can delay applications, backups, databases, and logging.
items.commonMistake: Only checking capacity instead of performance.

items.label: Network Pressure
items.description: How close the network path is to saturation.
items.whyItMatters: Network pressure can create latency, drops, and stalled transfers.
items.commonMistake: Assuming bandwidth is fine because the link is rated high.

items.label: Queueing
items.description: How much work is waiting instead of being processed immediately.
items.whyItMatters: Queueing usually means demand is exceeding service rate.
items.commonMistake: Looking only at average utilization and ignoring wait time.

items.label: Headroom
items.description: Spare capacity left above normal load.
items.whyItMatters: Headroom prevents the system from becoming unstable during bursts.
items.commonMistake: Running every resource close to its limit.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means no single resource appears dominant. Watch means one area deserves review. Risk means the system likely has a clear limiting resource that should be addressed first.
bullets: Healthy: the system appears to have enough margin based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the system may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: reduce load, add capacity, tune the system, or validate more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most performance planning problems come from trusting averages, ignoring peaks, or optimizing the wrong resource.
bullets: Using average values while ignoring peak or percentile behavior.
Fixing the easiest resource instead of the actual bottleneck.
Ignoring queueing, latency, and user impact.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built or tuned.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace load testing, monitoring, vendor guidance, application profiling, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Performance depends on workload behavior, not just hardware capacity.
Peak periods and tail latency matter.
A design with headroom is usually easier to operate and troubleshoot.
Real-world validation is still required for critical systems.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, application testing, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual workload and platform.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
